sources.title: IEC 60599:2022 — Mineral oil-filled electrical equipment in service — Guidance on the interpretation of dissolved and free gases analysis
IEEE C57.104-2019 — IEEE Guide for the Interpretation of Gases Generated in Mineral Oil-Immersed Transformers

meta.description: Structured representation of IEC 60599 and IEEE C57.104 transformer DGA interpretation standards. Intended for use by Aaron's PS B scenario generation pipeline. See data/knowledge/README.md for schema notes and consumption instructions.
meta.server_note: The fmsr_server analyze_dga tool implements the Rogers Ratio method from IEC 60599. Gas thresholds used for condition screening come from IEEE C57.104-2019. Scenario gas values should be consistent with these thresholds to produce realistic analyze_dga outputs.

iec_60599.description: IEC 60599 defines methods for interpreting dissolved gas analysis (DGA) in oil-filled transformers. The Rogers Ratio method is the primary fault classification tool.

rogers_ratio_method.description: Classifies fault type from three gas ratios. A set of gas readings that matches no row defaults to N (normal or inconclusive). This is the method implemented in the fmsr_server analyze_dga tool.

fault_table.label: Normal / Inconclusive
fault_table.description: Default result when gas ratios do not match any classified fault row. N is not a classified fault — it has no ratio ranges. Any gas combination that falls outside all other rows produces N. Do not attempt to target specific ratio ranges to generate an N scenario; instead use gas values from representative_gas_profiles.
fault_table.typical_cause: Normal insulation aging, or mixed fault signatures that do not cleanly match a single pattern

fault_table.label: Partial Discharge
fault_table.description: Low-level ionization without bridging, typically in voids or cavities.
fault_table.typical_cause: Voids in insulation, gas cavities, high-moisture paper

fault_table.label: Low-Energy Electrical Discharge
fault_table.description: Sparking or corona discharge in oil or between surfaces.
fault_table.typical_cause: High electric field stress, floating potential, turn-to-turn incipient fault

fault_table.label: High-Energy Electrical Discharge (Arcing)
fault_table.description: Power-follow arc, flashover, or sustained arcing between conductors.
fault_table.typical_cause: Insulation breakdown, winding short circuit, tap changer failure

fault_table.label: Thermal Fault — Low Temperature (<300°C)
fault_table.description: Mild local overheating, typically in solid insulation or mild conductor hot spot.
fault_table.typical_cause: Circulating currents, overloading, insulation contact with hot surface

fault_table.label: Thermal Fault — Medium Temperature (300–700°C)
fault_table.description: Local overheating in oil or conductor, significant thermal degradation.
fault_table.typical_cause: Core hot spot, conductor short circuit, cooling blockage

fault_table.label: Thermal Fault — High Temperature (>700°C)
fault_table.description: Severe thermal fault, carbonization of oil or paper.
fault_table.typical_cause: Core bolt fault, severe conductor overheating, cooling system failure

rogers_ratio_method.generator_note: IMPORTANT: Do not derive gas values from fault_table. The fault_table represents the IEC 60599 standard; the server implementation diverges on several rows (see server_rogers_table_note in representative_gas_profiles). Always use representative_gas_profiles as the source of gas values for scenario generation.

representative_gas_profiles.description: Pre-computed gas values (ppm) verified to produce each fault code from fmsr_server.analyze_dga. Use these as the basis for scenario gas inputs. Safe variation: ±10% per gas value preserves the target iec_code for all profiles. Do not use ±20% — several profiles sit close enough to ratio boundaries that larger variation flips the fault code.
representative_gas_profiles.server_rogers_table_note: The fmsr_server Rogers table ranges are: PD=(R1:[0.1,1), R2:[0,0.1), R3:[0,1)), D1=(R1:[0.1,1), R2:[0.1,3), R3:[0,1)), D2=(R1:[0.1,1), R2:[0.1,3), R3:[1,inf)), T1=(R1:[0.1,1), R2:[0,0.1), R3:[1,3)), T2=(R1:[1,3), R2:[0,0.1), R3:[1,3)), T3=(R1:[3,inf), R2:[0,0.1), R3:[3,inf)). Key divergences from IEC 60599: (1) PD and D1 — standard requires R1 in [0,0.1) (H2 strongly dominant); server uses R1 in [0.1,1). Profiles with H2=500, CH4=100 (R1=0.2) are correct for the server but would not match the standard PD/D1 rows. (2) D2 — standard requires R2>3 (high acetylene ratio); server uses R2 in [0.1,3) with R3>=1. (3) T1 — standard requires R1>1 (CH4 dominant over H2); server requires R1 in [0.1,1) (H2 dominant). These are opposite. Profiles below are calibrated to the server implementation, not the standard text.

D2.note: Server D2 = moderate-severity arc discharge (R2 in [0.1,3)). IEC standard D2 = high-energy arcing with R2>3. Use condition_4_trigger flag from fault_table for catastrophic arcing scenarios.

ieee_c57_104.description: IEEE C57.104-2019 defines a four-condition framework for evaluating transformer health from dissolved gas analysis. Condition 1 is normal; Condition 4 requires immediate action.

conditions.label: Caution — Investigate
conditions.action: Obtain laboratory sample, determine rate of gas generation, increase monitoring frequency.

conditions.label: Alert — Monitor Closely
conditions.action: Sample every 3–6 days. Investigate root cause. Consider offline inspection or planned outage.

conditions.label: Immediate Action
conditions.action: Consider de-energizing the transformer. Inspect, retest, or remove from service.

gas_thresholds_ppm.description: IEEE C57.104-2019 Table 1 — per-gas limits defining condition boundaries. Values are dissolved gas concentration in ppm. Exceeding a gas threshold moves the unit to that condition level for that gas; the overall condition is the worst across all gases.

TDCG.description: Total Dissolved Combustible Gas (sum of H2+CH4+C2H2+C2H4+C2H6+CO).

rate_of_generation.description: IEEE C57.104 emphasizes that rate of gas generation (ppm/day or ppm/month) is as important as absolute values. A rapidly rising Condition 2 unit may pose more risk than a stable Condition 3 unit.
rate_of_generation.escalation_rule: If the rate of increase doubles from one sample interval to the next, escalate by one condition level regardless of absolute value.

operational_context.description: Domain context for generating realistic scenario operating conditions. Drawn from IEEE C57.104 and utility practice documented in docs/scenario_realism_validation.md.

maintenance_decision_horizons.description: Typical time horizons for maintenance decisions by urgency level. Use these to set realistic response windows in work order scenarios.

triggers: rapid rate of gas generation (>2x prior interval)

work_order_minimum_fields.description: Minimum realistic fields for a corrective work order in a utility CMMS. Based on IEEE C57 practice and documented in docs/scenario_realism_validation.md.

asset_operating_context.description: Three factors that must be present in realistic multi-domain scenarios. Absence makes scenarios implausibly generic.

must_have_fields.impact: Determines acceptable risk tolerance and urgency of response
Directly affects whether de-energizing is feasible
Overloading accelerates thermal fault progression; affects urgency

scenario_generator_hints.description: Direct instructions for Aaron's generation pipeline. Maps scenario types to the specific fields and values that should be populated from this artifact.

fault_diagnosis_scenario.gas_values: Use a representative_gas_profiles entry for the target iec_code. Vary values ±10% to create distinct scenario instances. Do not exceed ±10% — some profiles sit close to ratio boundaries and larger variation flips the fault code.
fault_diagnosis_scenario.condition_level: Derive from ieee_c57_104.gas_thresholds_ppm — target Condition 2 or 3 for most scenarios (Condition 1 is trivially normal). Exception: faults with condition_4_trigger: true (D2, T3) naturally produce Condition 3–4 due to elevated C2H2 or C2H4; use Condition 4 gas values for those.
fault_diagnosis_scenario.example: For a D2 arcing scenario: use D2 profile from representative_gas_profiles (H2=500, CH4=100, C2H2=60, C2H4=120, C2H6=50). This gives R1=0.2, R2=0.5, R3=2.4, which the server maps to D2.

rul_risk_scenario.horizon_days: Use maintenance_decision_horizons to pick a realistic window. 30 days = Condition 3 post-investigation. 180 days = Condition 2 stable unit.
rul_risk_scenario.required_context: Include asset_criticality_tier, spare_availability, and current_loading_pct from asset_operating_context.
rul_risk_scenario.example: Forecast RUL for critical transformer at 95% load with no spare — use 30-day horizon, emergency urgency.

work_order_scenario.priority_selection: Map iec_code to urgency: D2/T3 → emergency, D1/T2 → high, PD/T1 → medium. Override if Condition 4 threshold breached.
work_order_scenario.fields: Always include all work_order_minimum_fields. Use work_types from operational_context.
work_order_scenario.example: D2 diagnosis + Condition 4 C2H2 → emergency work order, loto_reference required, task_steps include de-energize and inspect.

multi_domain_scenario.flow: IoT sensor readings → TSFM anomaly detection → FMSR fault classification → WO creation
multi_domain_scenario.gas_consistency: If scenario specifies DGA gases, ensure the same gas values appear in both the IoT sensor readings and any analyze_dga call. Use a representative_gas_profiles entry as the source of truth.
multi_domain_scenario.example: T-015 at 92% load, critical tier, no spare: IoT shows rising temperatures → TSFM detects anomaly → FMSR returns T2 → WO created with high priority and 1-week horizon.

variation_strategy.description: How to generate multiple distinct scenario instances from the same fault type.
variation_strategy.gas_variation: Vary each gas value ±10% of the representative profile. Do not exceed ±10% — some profiles sit close to ratio boundaries and larger variation flips the fault code.
variation_strategy.transformer_variation: Vary transformer ID (T-001 through T-030), loading percentage, criticality tier, and spare availability.
variation_strategy.context_variation: Vary one must_have_field per instance to ensure the scenarios probe different decision paths.
